items.new_candidate: Willie Joe Reese, DO (also: W.J. Reese)
items.evidence: The transcript context (SRT lines 919-939) shows Anderson describing the established older Black physician in Albany who, alongside attorney C.B. King, convinced him to settle his practice there in 1957; in the very next breath (SRT line 939) Anderson names 'William L. "Bill" Reese DO who was also in practice there' offering to take him into practice, and at SRT line 979 he again references the same older colleague phonetically as 'really joeries who I said was getting up an age.' Multiple independent authoritative sources confirm this person is Willie Joe Reese, DO — the only practicing African American osteopath in the South at that time. The New Georgia Encyclopedia explicitly states 'Reese, then the only practicing African American osteopath in the South, persuaded Anderson to apply to the Des Moines Still College of Osteopathy.' The American Osteopathic Association's publication The DO confirms 'William Reese, DO, in Albany, Georgia, who was the only African American DO in the entire southern U.S. at that time.' Both 'a Jorese' and 'joeries' are Whisper phoneticizations of 'Joe Reese' / 'W.J. Reese.' The Layer 5 candidate 'E.D. Jordan / E.D. "Joe" Jorisi' does not appear in any authoritative source on Anderson or Albany Black physicians of the 1950s and should be rejected.
